coredata.dc:title: Preparation and application of carbon dots with tunable luminescence by controlling surface functionalization
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Dong
Liu, Tiantian
Li, Shujun
Wang, Chunfeng
Zhuo, Kelei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using citric acid, potassium persulfate, and trishydroxymethylaminomethane as precursors. By adjusting reaction temperature, surface defects of the prepared CDs are modulated. The CDs emit green, yellow, and red fluorescence under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. In addition, the CDs are nearly spherical particles and have high stability in aqueous solution, and good biocompatibility, and thus have been applied successfully as a sensor to selectively detect Hg2+ and as a fluorescent bioimaging reagent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Surface defect
Tunable luminescence
Mercury ion
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) TEM, and HRTEM (inset) images, and (d–f) the size distribution histogram of CDs-100, CDs-130, and CDs-160.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD patterns, and (c–e) High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of CDs-100, CDs-130, and CDs-160.
UV spectra and FL spectra (Ex and Em) of (a) CDs-100, (b) CDs-130, and (c) CDs-160. Insets: photographs of the CDs under daylight (left) and an excitation of 365 nm (right).
FL spectra of (a) CDs-100, (b) CDs-130, and (c) CDs-160 at different excitation wavelengths.
Effect of (a) storage time, (b) ionic strength, (c) UV lamp irradiation, and (d) pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs.
(a) Effect of different metal ions on the FL intensity of CDs-100; (b) Linear detection of Hg2+ using CDs-100.

(a) Effect of CDs-100 on HepG2 cells viability; (b) intracellular FL intensity of HepG2 cells laser confocal imaging; (c) CDs-100 incubated HepG2 cells Laser confocal imaging; and (d) with Hg2+ for 0.5 h after incubation.
Preparation process of carbon dots at different temperatures.
Analysis data of C1s XPS spectra of the CDs.

head.ce:title: Preparation and application of carbon dots with tunable luminescence by controlling surface functionalization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan, 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals
Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan, 453007, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using citric acid, potassium persulfate, and trishydroxymethylaminomethane as precursors. By adjusting reaction temperature, surface defects of the prepared CDs are modulated. The CDs emit green, yellow, and red fluorescence under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. In addition, the CDs are nearly spherical particles and have high stability in aqueous solution, and good biocompatibility, and thus have been applied successfully as a sensor to selectively detect Hg2+ and as a fluorescent bioimaging reagent.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials are a new type of materials developed at the end of the twentieth century, and have been the focus of scientists' research due to their unique electrical, optical, and thermal properties. So far, the discovered carbon nanomaterials mainly include zero-dimensional fullerenes, carbon dots (CDs), one-dimensional carbon nanotubes, and two-dimensional graphene. Among them, CDs have many advantages in some fields of applications, such as ion detection [1–9], photocatalysis [10,11], bioimaging [12–16], optical devices [17–19], and drug delivery [20,21] due to their excellent unique properties: good water solubility, excellent optical stability, and strong chemical inertness [22–26]. Therefore, they have become a promising substitute for semiconductor quantum dots (QDs).

In recent years, CDs with tunable luminescence have good application prospects in the fields of biological imaging and optoelectronic devices. In particular, red-emitting fluorescent CDs are urgently needed because red light has a strong penetrating ability to promote deep tissue imaging. For example, Lin et al. synthesized three kinds of CDs with blue, green, and red light emission by changing the carbon source [27]. Fan et al. produced multicolor band gap fluorescence using citric acid and diaminonaphthalene as precursors [28]. Xiong et al. obtained full-color CDs by modulating π-electron system and graphitic nitrogen content [29]. However, their preparation process was time-consuming and complicated. Therefore, it is necessary to seek a simple method to prepare CDs with tunable luminescence.

Herein, we report a simple one-step synthesis of CDs with tunable luminescence using citric acid, potassium persulfate, and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (THAM) as precursors by adjusting the reaction temperature (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence from green to red color, excellent fluorescence stability, high resistance to photobleaching, tunable luminescence properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. The CDs can be used as the fluorescent probe to detect metal ion and the cell imaging reagent.
Materials, equipment, and cytotoxicity tests are shown in the Supporting Information (SI).

CDs were obtained by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2.1014 g of citric acid, 1.2114 g of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (THAM), and 2.7032 g of potassium persulfate were added 40 mL of deionized water and sonicated until the mixture was completely dissolved, and then the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 100 °C, 130 °C, or 160 °C for 1 h. After cooled to room temperature, the products were adjusted to neutral with sodium hydroxide and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove unreacted particles. The filtrates were dialyzed (MWCO: 3500 Da) for 20 h. The CDs were obtained from the above process. The prepared CDs at 100 °C, 130 °C, and 160 °C were marked as CDs-100, CDs-130, and CDs-160, respectively.

Common metal ions (Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Na+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Co2+) were used to evaluate the selectivity of the prepared CDs. Solutions of above metal ions (100 μL, 10−3 mol L−1) were respectively added to an aqueous CDs solution (100 μL, 0.1 g L−1), then the mixtures were diluted to 1.0 mL with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10−3 mol L−1) and shaken to obtain homogeneous solutions. After being stood for 5 min, the fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded at room temperature. In order to test the sensitivity of the CDs to Hg2+, different amounts of Hg2+ were respectively added to an aqueous CDs solution (100 μL, 0.1 g L−1). The rest of the process is the same as above.

HepG2 cells were seeded in a laser confocal dish in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum and incubated for 48 h. After 48 h, the cells were incubated with CDs-100 for 3 h. Then, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove excess CDs-100. The imaging was conducted using a single-photon laser scanning confocal microscope with excitation wavelengths at 405, 488, and 559 nm, respectively.

The morphology of the prepared CDs was measured by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a–c, all three kinds of CDs are uniform in size. The particle sizes of CDs-100, CDs-130, and CDs-160 are in the range of 1.00–3.50 nm, 2.50–5.50 nm, and 4.40–6.80 nm, and corresponding average diameters are 1.95, 3.58, and 5.51 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d–f). It is obvious that the temperature can affect the size distribution of the prepared CDs. HRTEM images (see insets of Fig. 1a–c) shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to (100) in-plane of graphene (100 facet) [30–32].

The functional groups of the prepared CDs were characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 2 a, FTIR spectra of three kinds of CDs show similar absorption peaks. The broad band at 3308-3154 cm−1is assign to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H [33–35]. These hydrophilic groups make the synthesized CDs dispersible well in water. The peak at 2925 cm−1 is ascribed to C–H bond [36]. At the same time, the peaks at 1570 cm−1 and 1388 cm−1 correspond to CO and COO-/C–S groups [15,37], respectively. The peak at 1048 cm−1 is caused by C–N, –SO3 -, and C–O–C groups [23,25].

XRD patterns (Fig. 2b) of all the three kinds of CDs show a broad peak at 19.1°, corresponding an interlayer spacing of 0.44 nm [38]. This suggests a highly disordered arrangement of carbon atoms in the CDs, which is attributable to the hetero-atom groups introduced during the synthesis of CDs. In addition, XPS was also used to confirm the surface elemental content of the CDs. Fig. S1a shows full spectra of the three kinds of CDs, indicating four elements: C1s (284.8eV), N1s (400.8eV), S2p (168.4eV), and O1s (531.5eV), and their elemental contents are listed in Table S1. The C1s XPS spectra of the CDs were all divided into five components: C–C/CC at 284.5 eV, C–S/C–N at 285.0 eV, C–O at 286.3 eV, –CO at 288.1 eV, and –COOH at 292.3 eV (Fig. 2c–e) [39–41]. Analytical results of C1s XPS spectra of the CDs are listed in Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 2c–e and Table 1, the content of carboxyl groups increases as the reaction temperature increases. It is clear that the higher the surface oxidation degree of the CDs, the larger surface defect degree of graphene network. The surface defects, which are mainly caused by the surface oxidation, serve as the capturing centers of excitons to give rise to the surface-oxidation-related fluorescence. Thus, the fluorescence emission peak of the prepared CDs moved to longer wavelengths with the increase of –COOH content, which is consistent with previous work [42–44]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s can be divided into three components centered at 399.5eV, 400.3eV, and 401.4eV, corresponding to C–N–C, C2–N–H, and (C)3–N chemical environments, respectively (see Fig. S1b) [40,45]. The high-resolution S2p XPS spectrum can be divided into two peaks at 163.4 and 168.1 eV, possibly arising from sulfur and sulfur oxygenation, respectively (Fig. S1c) [39,40]. These results from XPS are consistent with those from FTIR, suggesting that there were rich nitrogen and sulfur elements on the surface of the CDs.

The UV absorption spectra of the three kinds of CDs all present a distinct absorption peak at 270 nm, which corresponds to the n-π* electronic transition of CO (Fig. 3 a–c) [39,46]. In fluorescence spectra, the optimal emission wavelengths of CDs-100, CDs-130, and CDs-160 are 528, 570, and 652 nm, corresponding to the excitation wavelengths of 416, 458, and 522 nm, respectively (Fig. 3a–c). The results reveal that the reaction temperature can affect the fluorescence properties of the prepared CDs: a higher temperature leads to a longer emission wavelength of the CDs. The insets in Fig. 3a–c shows that the solutions of the three kinds of CDs all are clear in daylight. However, under UV light, the solutions of CDs-100, CDs-130, and CDs-160 are green, yellow, and red, respectively. The result shows that the temperature can affect the distribution of the emission wavelength of the prepared CDs. It is obvious that at a higher temperature, a higher degree of oxidation on the CD surfaces was obtained, which leads to a larger surface defect degree. Therefore, different wavelength emission fluorescence may be derived from the difference in the surface defects of the prepared CDs [42,43].

Similar with e as most carbon-based FL materials, the prepared CDs also exhibit an excitation-dependent FL emission behavior [14,22,47–49]. For the CDs-100, the FL spectra of CDs were obtained at the excitation wavelength from 410 nm to 550 nm (Fig. 4 a). The red shift of the emission peak indicates that the emission of the CDs depends on the excitation wavelength. The FL behavior for CDs-130 and CDs-160 are consistent with that for CDs-100 (Fig. 4b–c). The absolute quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized CDs was measured by integrating spheres. With the increase of temperature, the QY of the prepared CDs is gradually reduced: the QY of CDs-100, CDs-130, and CDs-160 is 2.61%, 2.25%, and 1.65%, respectively.

We explored further the FL stability of the CDs. Firstly, as shown in Fig. 5 a, there is almost no change in FL intensity of the CDs during a long-term at room temperature. Secondly, the CDs have basically the same FL intensity at the presence of NaCl (up to 1.0 M), indicating their excellent salt tolerance (Fig. 5b). Thirdly, with the increase of UV lamp irradiation time, the FL intensity of the CDs is hardly affected, suggesting that the CDs are high resistant to photobleaching (Fig. 5c). Finally, it can be observed from Fig. 5d that the FL intensity of the CDs changed when pH at 1.0–14.0. The results are consistent with those reported in the literature [12,23,25,37]. The FL lifetimes of the CDs were recorded by a single photon counting technique. As shown in Figs. S2a–c and Table S2, the calculated average FL lifetime of the CDs decreased with increasing reaction temperature.

The CDs as a FL sensor to selectively detect some metal ion were studied in the presence of different metal ions. In Fig. 6 a, Hg2+ significantly quenched the FL of the CDs. This observation can be attributed to that Hg2+ quenches the fluorescence of the CDs presumably via electron or energy transfer [50–52]. In addition, we also examined the interference of other metal ions (Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Na+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cr2+, Pb2+, and Ag+). Fig. S3 shows that the fluorescence of the CDs can be effectively quenched by Hg2+ in the presence of these interfering ions, and the fluorescence intensity is hardly affected by these interfering ions. This may be because the C–S bonds on the surface of the CDs have a stronger affinity for Hg2+ than for other metal ions [53]. As shown in Fig. 6b, when adding different amounts of Hg2+ to a dispersion of the CDs, two linear equations were obtained: F/F
0 = −0.00278c + 0.95969 (R = 0.99252) in a concentration range of 10–80 μM; F/F
0 = −0.00142c + 0.85122 (R = 0.99389) in 80–250 μM, with a detection limit of 0.67 μM according to 3δ/k, respectively. The analytical performances (linear range and detection limit) of the proposed method and other reported methods are collected in Table S3. It can be seen that the proposed method shows a wide linear range and acceptable detection limit.

To test the practical application of the proposed method, we used it to determine the recovery of Hg2+ in local river water samples (Weihe River) using the standard addition method. The recoveries of Hg2+ in the spiked samples were in the range of 98.4–102.2% (Table S4), showing little interference of some impurities present in the sample (possibly minerals, organics, bacteria, etc.), indicating the potential of the CDs in the practical applications.

Cytotoxicity is an indispensable parameter in vivo imaging application. The toxicity of CDs-100 to HepG2 cells was evaluated by MTT assay. The experimental results showed that the cell viability was higher than 85% after HepG2 cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs-100 (up to 1000 μg mL−1) for 48 h (Fig. 7 a), indicating that CDs-100 has low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Therefore, the prepared CDs can be used as a potential candidate for bioimaging applications. As shown in Fig. 7b, as the excitation wavelength increases, the intracellular FL intensity of the CDs decreases. After the addition of Hg2+, the FL intensity in the cells was greatly reduced. Fig. 7c shows a laser confocal microscopy image of HepG2 cells incubated with CDs-100 for 4 h HepG2 cells showed bright FL at different excitation wavelengths (405, 488, and 559 nm). After incubation with 90 μL Hg2+ for 0.5 h, the FL intensity of CDs in the cells significantly decreased (Fig. 7c–d). The multicolor emission of the CDs indicates that they can be used as a promising material for bioimaging.

In summary, we developed a facile method to synthesize CDs with tunable luminescence by one-step hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, potassium persulfate, and TRIS. The results showed that a higher temperature is in favor of the formation of the CDs with more carboxyl groups on their surfaces and longer wavelength of photoluminescence emissions. The formation of more carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs led to more surface defects, so the fluorescence emissions of the CDs shifted to a longer wavelength. At the same time, the prepared CDs also have excellent fluorescence properties and can be used as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of Hg2+. In addition, the low toxicity and good compatibility of CDs make it as a promising probe in biomedical and multicolor displays application.

Dong Sun: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Methodology. Tiantian Liu: Investigation, Software, Data curation, Formal analysis. Shujun Li: Methodology, Software. Chunfeng Wang: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Kelei Zhuo: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21873026, 21573058, U1504212).
